does: This paper presents WorldModelLens, an open-source interpretability framework that unifies interpretation methods across diverse world model architectures. Rather than reimplementing probing, activation patching, sparse autoencoders, and surprise analysis separately for each model type, the framework defines a shared capability-typed interface that abstracts over recurrent state-space models like PlaNet and Dreamer, token-based transformers like IRIS, and joint-embedding architectures like I-JEPA. The core contribution is identifying that despite substantial architectural differences in how these models compress observations—whether through recurrent latent states, learned codebooks, or latent space predictions—they share underlying computational primitives that can be unified through a typed adapter interface. This enables researchers to apply a single set of interpretability tools across heterogeneous world model implementations without repeated engineering effort.
connects: This work builds on two converging research streams: the rapid proliferation of diverse world model architectures designed for learning predictive models of visual environments, and the maturing field of mechanistic interpretability that has developed specialized tools like probing, activation patching, and sparse autoencoders initially for transformer language models. Prior work established that standard deep learning tooling like hooks and caching were tied to specific architectures, limiting interpretability research. Simultaneously, world models research has explored fundamentally different computational substrates—latent recurrent models, discrete token-based sequences, and implicit latent spaces—each optimizing different tradeoffs between compression, efficiency, and decode-ability. The paper recognizes that existing interpretability frameworks inherited assumptions from language model analysis and lack semantics for actions, environment rollouts, and imagination, creating unnecessary friction when researchers attempted to analyze world models.
enables: This work enables a new phase of comparative interpretability research across world model families, allowing systematic investigation of how different architectural choices affect what environmental structure and dynamics are learned. By providing standardized tooling, it reduces barriers to interpretability studies that would otherwise require substantial reimplementation, potentially accelerating discovery of shared principles in how different models represent environments. The capability-typed interface itself becomes a research artifact that could guide future world model designs toward interpretability-friendly primitives. Future work could extend this framework to cover new emerging model families, potentially developing model-agnostic interpretability methods that generalize across even broader categories of sequential prediction tasks. The infrastructure also enables empirical comparison of which interpretability methods are most effective for different architectural choices and prediction problems.
